---
name: project-relational-algebra-render
description: "Canonical instructor vocabulary for relational algebra rendering in txt2tex"
metadata: 
  node_type: memory
  type: project
  originSessionId: join-rename-mission-2026-05-23
---

## Canonical instructor vocabulary (slides/topic02.pdf §45)

The instructor uses these names consistently:

| Operator | Source keyword | LaTeX emit |
|----------|---------------|------------|
| Natural join | `join` | `\mathrm{Join}(R, S)` |
| Theta-join | `join [p]` | `\mathrm{Join}_{p}(R, S)` |
| Restriction | `sigma[p](R)` | `\mathrm{Restrict}_{p}(R)` |
| Projection | `pi[A, B](R)` | `\mathrm{Project}_{A, B}(R)` |
| Rename | `rho[A as B](R)` | `\mathrm{Rename}_{A \to B}(R)` |
| Division | `R div S` | `R \div S` |
| Group | `R group ({A} as m)` | `\mathop{\mathrm{GROUP}}` form |
| Count | `Count(attr)` | `\mathrm{Count}(attr)` |

## History and why this matters

The engine previously used `bowtie` as the source keyword and emitted `R \otimes S`.
Both were wrong:

1. `\otimes` (⊗) denotes the **degenerate** Cartesian-product case — when R and S
   have NO common attributes, `Join(R, S)` degenerates to `R ⊗ S`. Using `\otimes`
   for the general natural join was semantically incorrect.
2. `bowtie` is a LaTeX symbol name, not an English operator. Instructor PDFs and
   slides write `Join(r, s)` consistently.

**Corrected (2026-05-23):** Source keyword renamed to `join`; emit changed to
`\mathrm{Join}(R, S)`. If future-me wants to defend `\otimes` again, re-read
slides/topic02.pdf §45 first.

## Do not confuse with Z operators

In Z, `⊗` is not defined. The relational algebra operators (`join`, `sigma`,
`pi`, `rho`, `div`) emit *outside* any Z environment (as `\noindent$...$` math).
fuzz silently skips them. Never put algebra inside a `zed`/`axdef`/`schema` block.

  Task #147 — adopt the instructor's canonical relation-rename
  surface `R[NEW/OLD]` (slide 02-31, Z RM §3.11) and retire the

  This is a Tier-1 change (cross-cutting, Z notation feature, multiple

  Design must be agreed before implementation begins.

  **Fixed premises — do NOT re-litigate:**
  - `SchemaRename` and `RelationRename` are separate AST nodes.
    They model two distinct semantic operations (name substitution
    in a schema TYPE vs. attribute substitution in a relation VALUE).
    Output shape is not a reason to conflate them.
  - Direction `[NEW/OLD]` per Z RM §3.11 and topic02.pdf slide 31.
    The instructor's `x [b1 .. bn / a1 .. an]` defines b as new and
    a as old.
  - Jim's principle holds: no hidden engine-side operand reversal.
    Source notation maps directly to output.  If the source uses
    `[NEW/OLD]`, the output uses `[NEW/OLD]`.  If existing files use
    `rho[OLD as NEW](R)`, migrating to the new form is a one-time
    source rewrite, not a hidden translation.

  answering each open question below.  For each: state the
  recommended decision, the rationale, the rejected alternatives
  (with reasons), and any open prerequisites (e.g. jms ruling,
  empirical check).

  ## Q1 — Source-syntax decision

  What does relation rename look like in the user's `.txt` source
  going forward?  Choices to evaluate:

    (a) `R[NEW/OLD]` only.  Drop `rho[A as B](R)` entirely.
        Existing source files migrate (mechanical or manual).
    (b) `R[NEW/OLD]` canonical; `rho[A as B](R)` accepted as a
        deprecated alias that emits a lexer warning for one release.

  Recommend one.  Document migration cost: how many files in
  examples/ and the three course-ox-* working trees use `rho`?

  ## Q2 — Parser disambiguation

  When the lexer sees `Identifier [ Identifier1 / Identifier2 ]`,
  how does the parser decide whether to produce `SchemaRename` or
  `RelationRename`?

  Constraints:
  - The parser has no symbol table (no type info at parse time).
  - Both forms share lexical surface.

  Identify the context cues available:
  - Position in the document (top-level Z paragraph vs body
    expression).
  - Surrounding tokens (e.g. inside `pi[..](X)`, `sigma[..](X)`,
    `... join ...` argues for relation; bare-form at top level
    argues for schema).
  - Anything else.

  Recommend a disambiguation rule, with one or two cases that the
  rule must accept and one or two that it must reject.

  ## Q3 — Empirical fuzz check

  Run a small fuzz test (modelled on the bowtie/Join empirical from
  earlier in this session):
    - Write a minimal .tex with `R[b/a]` inside a `\where` clause,
      where `R` is declared as `T \rel T`.

  fuzz cannot typecheck the form in a Z paragraph, the engine must
  ensure `RelationRename` routes through inline math via
  `_DAT_EXPRESSION_TYPES`.

    - Rewrite rule: `rho[OLD as NEW](R)` -> `R[NEW/OLD]`.

      `pi[x](rho[a as b](R))`).
    - Estimate review burden: how many files, how many
      occurrences.

  Independent of #147's main thrust, the existing
  `_generate_schema_rename` docstring labels positions as
  `[old/new]` -- wrong per Z RM §3.11.  Note this for the

  list (one line each), and the consequences subsection.  This is

tool_input.content:
# Rename Design: Task #147 — `R[NEW/OLD]` Adoption

**Mission:** rename-design  
**Worker:** ghr  
**Date:** 2026-05-23  
**Status:** Design complete — awaiting jra/jms approval before implementation

---

## Q1 — Source-syntax decision

### Decision

Adopt `R[NEW/OLD]` as the sole surface syntax for relation rename. Drop `rho[A as B](R)` entirely. No deprecation alias — the DAT feature family has not shipped publicly, so there are no external users to protect.

### Rationale

The `rho[A as B](R)` form is an invented convention with no precedent in Z RM or the instructor's slides. The canonical `R[NEW/OLD]` form is exactly what appears on slide 02-31 and in Z RM §3.11. Using the canonical form removes a mental-model gap for students cross-referencing their slides.

### Migration scope

**examples/ (.txt source files only — .tex/.pdf are generated):**

| File | `rho` occurrences |
|---|---|
| `examples/14_relational_databases/algebra_basics.txt` | 4 |
| `examples/16_multi_decl_calculus/q2d_demo.txt` | 1 |
| **Total** | **5 occurrences in 2 files** |

**course-ox-dat/ (.txt source files):**

| File | `rho` occurrences |
|---|---|
| `exercises-worked/solutions-s1q1.txt` | 1 |
| `exercises-worked/solutions-s1q3.txt` | 1 |
| `exercises-worked/solutions1.txt` | 2 |
| `exercises-worked/solutions-s2q5.txt` | 1 |
| `exercises-worked/solutions2.txt` | 1 |
| `_archive_pre_verification/exercises-worked/ex1.txt` | 1 |
| `_archive_pre_verification/exercises-worked/solutions-ex1.txt` | 6 |
| **Total** | **13 occurrences in 7 files** |

**course-ox-sbm/:** 0 occurrences.  
**course-ox-sem/:** not scanned (permission boundary); expected 0 — sem exercises use Z schemas not relational algebra.

**Grand total: 18 `rho` occurrences across 9 source files.**

### Rewrite rule

```
rho[OLD as NEW](R)  →  R[NEW/OLD]
```

The rewrite is mechanical for simple cases. Composition cases require per-instance attention:

```
pi[x](rho[a as b](R))
```

becomes:

```
pi[x](R[b/a])
```

The inner `rho` rewrites first; the outer operator (`pi`, `sigma`, `join`) is unchanged. The rewrite is still mechanical — no semantic analysis required — but the nested structure means a single regex substitution on `rho[...](.*)` risks mismatching parentheses in deeply nested expressions.

**Recommendation:** implement the rewrite as a one-pass Python script rather than a raw regex, using the existing lexer/parser to locate and rewrite `Rename` AST nodes. Alternatively, manual review of 18 call sites is feasible given the small count. The implementor (rmh or jra directly) should decide which path.

**Edge cases found:**

1. `rho[bookId as id](Loan) join rho[bookId as id](Book)` — two `rho` calls on one line. Becomes `Loan[id/bookId] join Book[id/bookId]`. Safe.
2. `rho[tournament as tournamentId](pi[tournament](sigma[winner = 'Ali'](Match)))` — `rho` wraps a composed expression. Becomes `(pi[tournament](sigma[winner = 'Ali'](Match)))[tournamentId/tournament]`. The operand `R` is now a parenthesized subexpression — the parser must accept `Expr[NEW/OLD]` where `Expr` is not just an `Identifier`. This is a **design constraint for Q2** (see below).
3. `rho[avg as TotalScripts](B) join S` — `rho` as left operand of `join`. Becomes `B[TotalScripts/avg] join S`. Safe.
4. `B == rho[name as ship](Ship)` — in an abbreviation RHS. Becomes `B == Ship[ship/name]`. The `Rename` node would be replaced by a `RelationRename` node; the `_expression_contains_dat_construct` check already handles this path.

### Rejected alternatives

- **`rho[A as B](R)` canonical; `R[NEW/OLD]` alias** — reversed precedence. The slide form is canonical; the invented form should not survive.
- **Deprecation alias for one release** — no external users; alias adds complexity with zero benefit.

### Prerequisites

None. Decision is self-contained. Implementation can proceed once jra approves Q2 disambiguation strategy.

---

## Q2 — Parser disambiguation

### Decision

Use a **two-level disambiguation rule** based on parse context, not token lookahead alone.

**Rule A — `rho`-keyword path becomes `RelationRename`.**  
The existing `rho` keyword maps to `_parse_rename` → `Rename` AST node. After this task, the `rho` keyword is removed and the `Rename` node is renamed `RelationRename`. `RelationRename` is produced only when the parser is in a relational-algebra expression context (inside `pi[..](...)`, `sigma[..](...)`, `join`, `div`, `group`, etc. chains — or wherever `Rename` is produced today via the `_parse_atom` RHO branch).

**Rule B — postfix `[NEW/OLD]` on an `Identifier` in Z paragraph context produces `SchemaRename`.**  
The existing `_parse_postfix` → `_parse_schema_rename_or_generic` path already does this. No change needed for schema rename.

**Rule C — postfix `[NEW/OLD]` on an `Identifier` in DAT expression context produces `RelationRename`.**  
This is the new case. When `R[NEW/OLD]` appears in a context where `R` is known to be a relation (i.e., inside a relational algebra expression — as an argument to `pi`, `sigma`, a `join` operand, etc.) the parser must produce `RelationRename`, not `SchemaRename`.

### Context cues available (no symbol table)

The parser has no type information at parse time. Available structural cues:

1. **Enclosing paragraph type.** Top-level document items: `axdef`, `schema`, `gendef`, `zed` → schema context. `Abbreviation` whose RHS contains a relational construct → DAT context (already detected via `_expression_contains_dat_construct`). Bare expression at top level is ambiguous.

2. **Immediately enclosing operator.** If the bracketed expression is an argument to `sigma`, `pi`, `rho` (being retired), `join`, `div`, `group`, `ungroup` — it is a relation. If it is a bare schema inclusion in a `schema` declaration list — it is a schema.

3. **Prior token.** `Identifier[NEW/OLD]` where the identifier is the direct operand of a relational operator is a relation. `Identifier[NEW/OLD]` at the top of a `schema` declaration section is a schema inclusion (schema rename).

### Recommended rule

**Use parse context, not lookahead alone.** The parser already tracks whether it is in a Z paragraph or a DAT expression (the `_expression_contains_dat_construct` gate). Extend this:

- Add a `_in_relational_context` flag to the parser state (or a context parameter threaded through `_parse_atom`/`_parse_postfix`). Set it `True` when entering any relational operator argument position (inside `pi`, `sigma`, `join` operand, `group` operand, etc.).
- In `_parse_postfix`, when the scanner sees `Identifier[slash-contents]`:
  - If `_in_relational_context` is True → produce `RelationRename`.
  - Otherwise → produce `SchemaRename` (existing behavior).

**Cases the rule must accept:**

- `pi[x](R[b/a])` — `R[b/a]` inside a `pi` argument: `_in_relational_context=True` → `RelationRename`. Correct.
- `schema T; S[a/b]; end` — `S[a/b]` as a schema inclusion: `_in_relational_context=False` → `SchemaRename`. Correct.

**Cases the rule must reject (parse error or wrong node):**

- `R[b/a]` bare at top level in a `zed` block — ambiguous. The fuzz empirical (Q3) confirms fuzz rejects `R[b/a]` on a relation in a `where` clause anyway, so this surface is only meaningful as a schema rename (existing behavior). The rule correctly produces `SchemaRename` here.
- `S[a/b]` where `S` is a relvar inside `sigma[...](S[a/b])` — rule correctly produces `RelationRename`.

### Additional constraint from Q1 edge case

The rewrite of `rho[tournament as tournamentId](pi[...](sigma[...](Match)))` produces `(pi[...](sigma[...](Match)))[tournamentId/tournament]`. The base of the rename is a parenthesized compound expression — an `FunctionApp`-style grouping, not a bare `Identifier`. The `_parse_postfix` gate (`isinstance(base, (Identifier, GenericInstantiation))`) blocks this path. The implementation must extend `RelationRename` to accept any `Expr` as its base, and `_parse_postfix` must be extended to allow `[NEW/OLD]` postfix on any expression, not just `Identifier`. This is a non-trivial parser change. **Flag for jra review** before implementation begins.

### Rejected alternatives

- **Pure lookahead / slash-only detection** — already used for `SchemaRename`. The problem is that both schema rename and relation rename have the same token sequence. A slash detector alone cannot distinguish them.
- **Keyword-prefix form `rename R[b/a]`** — adds a keyword, diverges from canonical Z RM form.
- **Symbol table at parse time** — requires a two-pass parse or deferred name resolution. Too heavy for this codebase.

### Prerequisites

- **jms ruling required:** Is `R[b/a]` where `R` is a relation ever valid in a Z paragraph (axdef `where` clause, zed block, etc.)? The fuzz empirical (Q3) says fuzz rejects it. If jms confirms it is never valid Z, then disambiguating by `_in_relational_context` is provably safe. If jms says it can be valid Z in some contexts, a more nuanced rule is needed.
- **jra design decision required:** Whether to extend `_parse_postfix` to allow `[NEW/OLD]` on arbitrary `Expr` bases (for the compound-operand case), or restrict migration to forms where the rename base is a bare identifier and document the limitation.

---

## Q3 — Empirical fuzz check

### Test performed

Wrote `/Users/jfreeman/Coding/txt2tex/.tmp/rename_fuzz_test.tex` containing:

```latex
\begin{axdef}
  T : \power \nat \\
  R : T \rel T
\where
  R[b/a] = \emptyset
\end{axdef}
```

Ran `fuzz rename_fuzz_test.tex` with `fuzz.sty` from `src/txt2tex/latex/`.

### Result

**Fuzz rejects the form.** Exit code 1. Error:

```
"rename_fuzz_test.tex", line 9: Syntax error at symbol "/"
```

Fuzz does not recognize `R[b/a]` as a valid expression on a relation value inside a Z paragraph. The `/` token is not part of the fuzz expression grammar in this position.

### Companion check: SchemaRename in schema inclusion

Wrote `schema_rename_fuzz_test2.tex`:

```latex
\begin{schema}{T}
  S[a/b]
\end{schema}
```

Fuzz accepts this cleanly (exit 0, no output). Schema rename is valid in a schema inclusion context.

### Implication

`RelationRename` cannot be emitted inside a Z environment (`axdef`, `schema`, `zed`, `gendef`). The existing `_DAT_EXPRESSION_TYPES` mechanism already routes `Rename` nodes through inline math (`\noindent$...$`) rather than Z environments. `RelationRename` (the new node replacing `Rename`) must remain in `_DAT_EXPRESSION_TYPES` with the same routing.

The rendered LaTeX form for `RelationRename` is not yet settled — see Q6. Current `Rename` renders as `\mathrm{Rename}_{OLD \to NEW}(R)`. This must be reconsidered against the instructor's notation (see Q6 / jms input needed).

### Rejected alternatives

No alternatives — empirical check is deterministic.

### Prerequisites

None — the fuzz result is conclusive.

---

## Q4 — Migration plan

### Scope

**18 `rho` occurrences across 9 source files** (see Q1 for per-file breakdown).

No `rho` in course-ox-sbm. course-ox-sem was not scanned (permission boundary) but is expected to have 0 occurrences.

### Rewrite rule

```
rho[A as B](R)  →  R[B/A]
```

Direction: OLD name `A` → NEW name `B`. In the new notation, NEW precedes OLD: `[B/A]` = `[NEW/OLD]`.

Multi-pair: `rho[A as B, C as D](R)` → `R[B/A, D/C]`.

### Mechanicity assessment

**Mostly mechanical; one structural exception.**

For all simple cases (bare identifier as the relation operand), the rewrite is a one-pass transformation:

```python
# Pseudocode — not a production regex
re.sub(
    r'rho\[([^\]]+)\]\(([^)]+)\)',
    lambda m: rewrite_rho(m.group(2), m.group(1)),
    source
)
```

where `rewrite_rho` parses the `"A as B, C as D"` pairs and outputs `R[B/A, D/C]`.

**Exception — `q2d_demo.txt` line 38:**

```
rho[tournament as tournamentId](pi[tournament](sigma[winner = 'Ali'](Match))))
```

The operand of `rho` is itself a compound expression (`pi[...](sigma[...](Match))`). The new form would be:

```
(pi[tournament](sigma[winner = 'Ali'](Match)))[tournamentId/tournament]
```

This requires the parser to accept `Expr[NEW/OLD]` where `Expr` is parenthesized. Whether the parser supports this is a Q2 implementation decision. If not supported in v1 of the implementation, this one call site must be rewritten as a two-step abbreviation:

```
T == pi[tournament](sigma[winner = 'Ali'](Match))
... T[tournamentId/tournament] ...
```

This is the only such case in the scanned files.

### Review burden

- **examples/:** 2 files, 5 call sites. Simple. All are bare-identifier operands. Mechanical.
- **course-ox-dat/exercises-worked/:** 5 files, 6 call sites. Mix of simple and one compound (see above). Mostly mechanical.
- **course-ox-dat/_archive/:** 2 files, 7 call sites. Archive — may not need migration if archive is not built.
- **Total review time estimate:** 1–2 hours, including running `make check` after each file.

### Migration order

1. Update engine first (add `RelationRename` node, wire parser, update `_DAT_EXPRESSION_TYPES`).
2. Migrate `examples/` — these are checked by CI.
3. Migrate `course-ox-dat/exercises-worked/` — these are the live courseware files.
4. Decide on archive files separately.

### Rejected alternatives

- **Automated regex migration** — safe for 17 of 18 call sites. The compound-operand case in `q2d_demo.txt` would produce syntactically broken output, so full automation without AST-level rewriting carries risk.

### Prerequisites

- Q2 decision on whether compound-operand `Expr[NEW/OLD]` is supported in v1.

---

## Q5 — Schema-rename docstring correction

### Issue

Two documentation artifacts label the `SchemaRename` pair direction incorrectly:

**`ast_nodes.py`, `SchemaRename` node (line 453):**
```python
pairs: list[tuple[str, str]]  # (old_name, new_name) pairs, at least one
```
The examples show `S[a/b]  -> pairs=[("a", "b")]` — labelled as `(old, new)`.

**`latex_gen.py`, `_generate_schema_rename` docstring (line 2241–2249):**
```
Renders ``S[old/new, ...]`` in math mode.
```
and example `S[a/b]   → S[a/b]`.

**Per Z RM §3.11:** `S[new/old]` — the new name appears first. If `S[a/b]` means `a` renames to `b`, then `a` is the new name and `b` is the old name. The docstrings have the labels backwards.

### Fix required

In the implementation mission, the implementor must:

1. In `ast_nodes.py`, change the pair tuple label from `(old_name, new_name)` to `(new_name, old_name)`, and update all docstring examples accordingly.
2. In `latex_gen.py`, change the generator variable names from `old, new` to `new_name, old_name` (or `new, old`) and update the docstring to read `S[new/old, ...]`.
3. Verify that all existing test cases that use `SchemaRename` still pass — the actual output bytes do not change (the generator just outputs `pairs[0]/pairs[1]`), but the semantic labeling becomes correct.

### Note on `RelationRename`

The new `RelationRename` node should be labeled correctly from the start: `pairs: list[tuple[str, str]]  # (new_name, old_name) per Z RM §3.11`.

### Rejected alternatives

- **Leave the docstring wrong** — violates the project's "documentation is the product" standard. The mislabeling will confuse implementors reading the code.

### Prerequisites

None. This is a documentation-only fix bundled with the implementation mission.

---

## Q6 — DESIGN.md ADR outline

### ADR skeleton

---

**ADR §N — Relation Rename: adopt `R[NEW/OLD]` canonical form, retire `rho[A as B](R)`**

**Context**

txt2tex originally invented a prefix-function notation for relation rename: `rho[A as B](R)`. This was modelled loosely on the `sigma`/`pi` prefix style but has no precedent in Z RM or the instructor's DAT slides. Topic02.pdf slide 31 uses the standard Z postfix form `x [b/a]` (Z RM §3.11), where NEW precedes OLD. Using the canonical form removes a translation step for students.

The `rho` keyword has not been published in any external release; the DAT feature family is internal only. No migration compatibility constraint applies.

**Decision**

Adopt `R[NEW/OLD]` as the sole surface form. Remove the `rho` keyword from the lexer. Add a `RelationRename` AST node distinct from `SchemaRename`. The parser distinguishes them by relational context (see below). `RelationRename` routes through `_DAT_EXPRESSION_TYPES` (inline math, not Z environments) because fuzz rejects the `[/]` form on relation values inside Z paragraphs.

**Rejected alternatives**

- `rho[A as B](R)` canonical, `R[NEW/OLD]` alias — reverses precedence; invented form should not survive.
- Single `SchemaRename` node for both cases — the semantic operations are distinct (schema type substitution vs. relation attribute substitution). Conflating them would break `_expression_contains_dat_construct` routing.
- Shared `rename` keyword (`rename R [b/a]`) — adds a keyword, diverges from Z RM.
- Two-pass parse with symbol table for disambiguation — too heavy; context flag is sufficient.

**Consequences**

- Positive: surface syntax matches Z RM §3.11 and instructor slides exactly.
- Positive: removes an invented keyword (`rho`) that confused the schema-rename keyword (which does not exist as a keyword — it is postfix syntax).
- Positive: `SchemaRename` and `RelationRename` are clearly separated; the `_DAT_EXPRESSION_TYPES` routing is unchanged.
- Neutral: `_generate_rename` (for `Rename`, now `RelationRename`) continues to emit `\mathrm{Rename}_{OLD \to NEW}(R)` — the rendered LaTeX form may need to be revisited against the instructor's typographic conventions (open question: does the instructor use `R[b/a]` directly in slides, or a named rename operator?).
- Negative: 18 existing source files require migration. Compound-operand cases (`rho[..](compound_expr)`) may require parser extension or two-step rewrite.
- Negative: docstring labels in `SchemaRename` and `_generate_schema_rename` are currently wrong (`old/new` instead of `new/old`); the implementation mission must fix these alongside the new `RelationRename` work.

---

## Ready-for-implementation checklist

The following items must be resolved before the implementation mission is drafted.

### Requires jms ruling

- [ ] **Q2/Q3 — Is `R[b/a]` on a relation ever valid in a Z paragraph context?** Fuzz says no (syntax error). If jms confirms this is categorically invalid Z, the `_in_relational_context` disambiguation is provably safe and complete. If jms identifies a counter-case, the disambiguation rule must be revised.
- [ ] **Q6 — LaTeX rendering of `RelationRename`.** Does the instructor expect `\mathrm{Rename}_{OLD \to NEW}(R)` (current `Rename` output) or a different form (e.g., `R[b/a]` in inline math)? Consult topic02 slides for rendered examples.

### Requires jra decision

- [ ] **Q2 — Compound-operand `RelationRename`.** Should v1 of the implementation support `Expr[NEW/OLD]` where `Expr` is a compound expression (not just an `Identifier`)? @dataclass(frozen=True)
class SchemaRename(ASTNode):
    """Schema renaming node (Z RM §3.11).

    Represents ``S[new/old, ...]`` — produce a schema identical to S except
    that each component named ``old`` is renamed to ``new``.

    Per Z RM §3.11 the NEW name appears first, the OLD name second.

    The schema expression is typically a plain (possibly decorated) Identifier.
    The ``pairs`` list holds ``(new_name, old_name)`` tuples in source order;
    at least one pair is required.

    Examples:
    - S[a/b]         -> schema=Identifier("S"),  pairs=[("a", "b")]  # a is new, b is old
    - S[a/b, c/d]    -> schema=Identifier("S"),  pairs=[("a", "b"), ("c", "d")]
    - S'[a/b]        -> schema=Identifier("S'"), pairs=[("a", "b")]
    - S[a'/b]        -> schema=Identifier("S"),  pairs=[("a'", "b")]

    Disambiguation from GenericInstantiation: the parser scans the bracket
    contents at depth 0 before committing.  If any ``/`` token appears, this
    node is constructed; otherwise GenericInstantiation is constructed.

    LaTeX rendering: schema_repr[new/old, ...]
    """

    schema: Expr  # Schema reference (typically a decorated Identifier)
    pairs: list[tuple[str, str]]  # (new_name, old_name) pairs per Z RM §3.11, at least one

// Relational Algebra Operators
// Demonstrates: sigma, pi, rename (R[new/old]), join (renders as ⊗), div.

TITLE: Relational Algebra
AUTHOR: txt2tex example

given Title, Author, MemberName, Genre

schema Book
  bookId : N
  title : Title
  author : Author
  genre : Genre
  pages : N
  year : N
end

schema Member
  memberId : N
  name : MemberName
end

schema Loan
  loanId : N
  bookId : N
  memberId : N
  dueYear : N
end


=== Restriction (sigma) ===

// Select tuples satisfying a predicate.

sigma[pages >= 500](Book)

// Nested restriction (chain predicates):

sigma[year >= 1990](sigma[pages >= 500](Book))


=== Projection (pi) ===

// Keep only named attributes.

pi[title, author](Book)

// Project after restrict:

pi[title, author](sigma[pages >= 500](Book))


=== Renaming (R[new/old]) ===

// Rename one attribute before joining (Z RM §3.11: new/old order).

pi[id, title, author, genre, pages, year](Book[id/bookId])

// Multiple renames at once:

pi[id, writer, genre, pages, year](Book[id/bookId, writer/author])


=== Natural Join and Theta-Join (join) ===

// Natural join — joins on attributes with matching names.

Loan join Book

// Rename then join (natural join on matching id):

pi[id, memberId, dueYear, title, author, genre, pages, year](Loan[id/bookId] join Book[id/bookId])

// Theta-join with explicit predicate:

Member join [Member.memberId = Loan.memberId] Loan


=== Division (div) ===

// Relational division — tuples in R associated with every tuple in S.

Loan div Book


=== Combined Query ===

// Titles and authors of books over 500 pages.

pi[title, author](sigma[pages >= 500](Book))

---

## Relation Rename (`R[NEW/OLD]`) and Fuzz

### Fuzz rejects `R[NEW/OLD]` inside Z paragraphs

**Standard Z Notation (Z RM §3.11)**: `R[b/a]` is valid relational rename
syntax — `b` is the NEW attribute name, `a` is the OLD attribute name.

**Fuzz**: Inside a `\begin{zed}`, `\begin{axdef}`, or `\begin{schema}`
environment, fuzz parses `[...]` on an expression as a generic instantiation.
A `/` at bracket depth 0 is not part of any Z grammar rule fuzz recognises in
those contexts, so fuzz rejects the expression.

**txt2tex decision**:

- `R[NEW/OLD]` relational rename is classified as a `RelationRename` AST node,
  which is included in `_DAT_EXPRESSION_TYPES` alongside `Restrict`, `Project`,
  `Join`, etc.
- When a `RelationRename` node appears anywhere in an expression (including as
  an abbreviation RHS), the engine routes the entire abbreviation through inline
  math (`\noindent$...$`) instead of `\begin{zed}`.
- Fuzz never sees the `/` — it only processes the Z-paragraph content.

**User syntax**:

```text
✅ CORRECT: pi[id, isbn](Book[id/bookId])      → inline math, fuzz skips it
✅ CORRECT: B == pi[ship, class](Ship[ship/name])  → inline math, fuzz skips it
❌ WRONG:   axdef ... Book[id/bookId] ... end  → fuzz rejects /
```

**Context requirement**: `[NEW/OLD]` is only recognised as a relation rename
inside a *relational context* (argument to `sigma`, `pi`, or right operand of
`join`/`div`).  At the top level of an abbreviation RHS, wrap in `pi` to force
the context:

```text
// Correct: pi forces relational context so [ship/name] is RelationRename
B == pi[ship, class, launched](Ship[ship/name])
```

**Reference**: `latex_gen.py` — `_DAT_EXPRESSION_TYPES`; `parser.py` —
`_in_relational_context` flag.
